name: Copper Mountain
short_description: BLM public rockhounding on Wyoming's Copper Mountain with excellent chrysocolla and malachite specimens. Flat, accessible terrain ideal for collectors of all levels.
description:
Copper Mountain in Fremont County offers rockhounders access to quality copper oxide minerals on BLM public land without permit requirements. The site features secondary copper minerals including bright green malachite and blue-green chrysocolla weathered from primary copper deposits. The flat terrain and proximity to the Sweetwater River valley make this an accessible destination for both beginners and experienced collectors.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province's rich mineral heritage. Miocene-aged porphyry copper systems created the primary ore bodies, and weathering processes over millions of years produced the vibrant secondary minerals visible at surface today. Chrysocolla and malachite form in oxidized zones above deeper sulfide deposits, making surface prospecting productive.

Collectors typically work the weathered outcrop areas and surrounding scree slopes where specimens weather free from the host rock. The flat topography means you can cover significant ground efficiently. Finding hand-sized specimens is common, though the 25-pound daily personal use limit applies per FLPMA regulations.

The area receives moderate rockhounding pressure, so success depends on thorough searching of exposed mineralized zones. Work the color - brilliant malachite green and chrysocolla blue stand out distinctly against the gray country rock. Smaller specimens often show the best color saturation and crystal detail.
history: Copper Mountain sits within the Wyoming portion of the Basin and Range province, where Tertiary porphyry copper systems developed during regional extension and magmatism. The weathering of these copper-rich rocks over 20+ million years created surface deposits of secondary minerals like malachite and chrysocolla that rockhounders collect today.
tips: Search the weathered outcrop areas and loose scree on hillsides for free specimens; bright green and blue minerals stand out clearly. Bring a small chisel or hammer to work specimens from softer weathered rock, and a 5x loupe to examine color gradations and crystal details. Focus on areas where you see color staining on exposed rock faces rather than random hillside searching. Pack out exactly 25 pounds or less - weigh specimens before leaving to stay compliant with BLM limits.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain at approximately 6,800 feet elevation. Scattered sagebrush and low vegetation typical of high desert. Well-defined paths between vehicle parking and collecting areas. Walking distance from parking is typically less than half a mile. Footing is stable on hard-packed ground with minimal loose rock hazards.
best_season: May - September. Higher elevation (6,800 ft) keeps this site cool during summer months. Spring and early summer offer best weather with stable ground conditions. Winter snow can make access difficult; late fall presents muddy conditions after precipitation.
written_directions: From Lander, take WY 131 north approximately 35 miles toward Dubois. Near milepost 45, turn east on the marked BLM access road toward Copper Mountain. Follow this rough dirt road for approximately 3 miles to the main parking area. The road is passable for high-clearance vehicles in dry conditions; check with local BLM office for current conditions after precipitation.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road with washboard texture. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Not suitable for low-clearance sedans in wet conditions.
parking_notes: Informal pullout area with capacity for 4-6 vehicles. No developed facilities or shade structures. Park near the base of the outcrop area.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. No removal of mineral specimens from active mining claims or posted private property. Collect only loose or naturally weathered specimens.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collection
25 lbs per day maximum
No motorized excavation
No power tools
No removal from active mining claims
Collect only loose or weathered material
nearest_services: Lander, Wyoming - approximately 40 miles southwest. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most fitness levels. Unpaved access road to parking area. Short walks (under half mile) to productive collecting spots. Stable footing on hardpacked ground. Minimal elevation gain required for collecting.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Copper Mountain near Lander, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Copper Mountain is known for secondary copper minerals including chrysocolla (bright blue-green), malachite (deep green with banding), and associated quartz and chalcedony specimens. These minerals formed from the weathering of primary copper deposits and are commonly found as loose fragments in the scree and on exposed outcrop surfaces.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Copper Mountain?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Copper Mountain sits on BLM public land where personal use rockhounding is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You simply need to follow the 25-pound daily collection limit and avoid any posted mining claims.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Copper Mountain for rockhounding?
faq.answer: May through September offers the best conditions. The high elevation (6,800 feet) keeps summers cooler than lower desert rockhounding sites. Winter snow and muddy roads in late fall make access difficult. Late May through early September typically provides stable ground and reliable weather.

faq.question: How far is Copper Mountain from town and what tools should I bring?
faq.answer: Copper Mountain is approximately 40 miles northeast of Lander, Wyoming. Bring a small hammer or chisel to work specimens from softer weathered rock, a 5x loupe to examine crystal detail, a sturdy bag or backpack, and a scale to weigh specimens before leaving to stay within the 25-pound daily limit.

faq.question: Is Copper Mountain rockhounding site suitable for families and children?
faq.answer: Yes, Copper Mountain is family-friendly and beginner-suitable. The flat terrain requires minimal hiking, and vibrant green and blue minerals are easy for children to spot. The site can be collected in a few hours, making it ideal for families with younger children.

faq.question: What vehicle do I need and what are road conditions like to Copper Mountain?
faq.answer: A passenger vehicle with high-clearance can reach the site in dry conditions, though an ATV or 4x4 is recommended. The access road is rough dirt with washboard texture. Contact the local BLM office for current road conditions after heavy rain or snow, as the rough road may become impassable.

meta_description: Find chrysocolla and malachite at Copper Mountain, Wyoming. BLM public land, no permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Easy terrain near Lander.
